title: Listening - Part 4: A Community Bicycle Workshop
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Dan, who runs a bicycle repair workshop. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Dan Williams, who repairs bicycles and runs classes in a community workshop. Dan, were bikes always your main interest? || Dan: I cycled as a teenager, but mainly because it was cheaper than taking the bus. I later worked in an electronics shop. When the shop closed, I had time to volunteer at a project that repaired unwanted bikes. [24]I discovered I loved solving mechanical problems and then seeing somebody ride away on something I had fixed[/24]. That led me to train professionally. || Presenter: Why does your workshop accept old bicycles? || Dan: Many need less work than people imagine: new brake cables, tyres or a careful clean. We repair and sell some at affordable prices, and donate others. [25]A bike that would otherwise be thrown away can give someone an inexpensive way to travel to work or college[/25]. That combination of reducing waste and helping people is central to us. || Presenter: Are modern bicycles difficult to repair? || Dan: Some are, especially electric models, which require specific diagnostic tools. But the most common repair has not changed much. [26]Many customers come in with gears that no longer work smoothly because the bike has been left outside and not maintained[/26]. A little regular care would prevent many expensive repairs. || Presenter: You run beginners’ maintenance classes. What do people learn? || Dan: We show them how to check tyres and brakes, repair a puncture and decide when professional help is needed. People often arrive saying they are no good with tools. [27]The important change is that they leave confident enough to deal with a simple problem rather than abandon a journey[/27]. That independence is what the class is really about. || Presenter: Has interest in cycling changed recently? || Dan: Yes, partly because more cycle lanes have been built locally. However, some people buy a bicycle and stop riding after one uncomfortable journey. [28]I recommend borrowing or hiring different types of bike before spending money, since the right size and riding position make an enormous difference[/28]. A fashionable model is not necessarily the right one. || Presenter: What is the greatest challenge for your business? || Dan: People assume a repaired second-hand bicycle should be extremely cheap. They do not see the hours of work or the cost of safe replacement parts. [29]The hardest thing is charging a fair price while keeping our bikes affordable for the people the project aims to help[/29]. We have to balance both sides carefully. || Presenter: What would you like to do next? || Dan: Schools teach children to ride, but teenagers often lack a bike that fits or know-how to maintain it. [30]I hope to develop workshops where teenagers repair donated bikes which they can then keep and use[/30]. They would gain both practical skills and transport.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What attracted Dan to bicycle repair?

options.A: The satisfaction of solving problems for riders.
options.B: The chance to continue working in electronics.
options.C: A desire to find a cheaper way to travel.

questions.explanation: Dan says he enjoyed fixing a mechanical problem and seeing somebody ride away on the repaired bike.

questions.question: Why does Dan's workshop restore old bicycles?

options.A: They are simpler to repair than new bikes.
options.B: They can provide affordable transport for people.
options.C: They are sold to fund college courses.

questions.explanation: Repaired bikes help people travel to work or college without spending too much.

questions.question: What common problem does Dan mention?

options.A: Electric bikes require tools that the workshop does not own.
options.B: People damage gears by trying to fix them at home.
options.C: Lack of care causes many bicycles' gears to work badly.

questions.explanation: He says gears often stop working smoothly after bikes are left outside and not maintained.

questions.question: What does Dan value most about the maintenance classes?

options.A: Participants gain confidence with basic repairs.
options.B: Participants learn when to buy a new bike.
options.C: Participants become interested in working as mechanics.

questions.explanation: He wants people to feel able to deal with simple problems instead of giving up a trip.

questions.question: What advice does Dan give to somebody buying a bicycle?

options.A: Choose a model designed for cycle lanes.
options.C: Avoid spending too much on comfort.

questions.explanation: Dan recommends hiring or borrowing types of bike first to find an appropriate size and position.

questions.question: What is the biggest difficulty for Dan's workshop?

options.B: Explaining how long each repair takes.
options.C: Setting prices that are both fair and affordable.

questions.explanation: He says balancing fair payment for the work with affordability is hardest.

questions.question: What does Dan hope to offer teenagers?

options.A: Lessons in riding safely to school.
options.B: A chance to repair bicycles for their own use.
options.C: Work experience in a professional bicycle shop.

questions.explanation: He plans workshops where teenagers repair donated bikes and then keep them.
